Data I/O for Web Applications

Data I/O for Web Applications

In web applications, it is possible for the client to tamper with data, so data from the client should never be trusted. Failure to properly validate input from the client or the environment is a security weakness.

To address this weakness, you should include data integrity checks wherever data passes between a trusted and a less-trusted boundary, for example between the application and the user’s web browser. Since input data cannot be trusted, it must be validated on every tier, checking for issues that are specific to the server executing the code in that tier.

The type of integrity control (checksum, HMAC, encryption, digital signature) should be related to the risk of the data transiting the boundary. Uniface supports encryption and decryption, so you can use $encode and $decode to manually code these checks.

Uniface protects non-editable fields using a salted MD5 hash and will automatically trigger a security error upon a webload if these have been tampered with. The hash code is calculated and transmitted with the field. When the client submits the page, for non-editable fields the hash value is recalculated for the submitted value and compared to the transmitted hash value. The hash value is calculated using a salted MD5 algorithm.

Threats

  • Interpreter Injection
  • Cross-Site Scripting
  • Command execution (buffer overruns)—buffer overruns (in which a program overruns a buffer's boundary while writing data to it, and overwrites adjacent memory) can be exploited to inject malicious code or corrupt data.
  • Session replay—using a session ID, an attacker can replay a web session to masquerade as a user and gain access to a web application.

Guidelines

The general strategy to defend against threats via data input is to:

  • Accept known good input, reject known bad input. For example, if you expect a telephone number, validate for a valid telephone number. If you don’t expect spaces, reject strings containing them, using ProcScript in the field's validate trigger.
  • Sanitize input by either eliminating or translating unwanted characters. For example, remove or translate unwanted quotes in a string.
  • Ensure that data is strongly typed and minimize the length. Before using submitted data:
    • Check the length.
    • Check the range (if numeric).
    • Ensure the data is signed or unsigned, as required.
    • Check the syntax or grammar.
  • Verify unprocessed client input before using it to create a hyperlink from it.
  • Use preDeserialize and postDeserialize triggers to secure dynamic changes made in the preSerialize trigger.

Related Links
  • trigger preDeserialize

    System trigger that processes an occurrence before it is loaded from a disconnected record set (XML, JSON, or Struct) into a component.

  • trigger postDeserialize

    System trigger that processes an occurrence after it is loaded from a disconnected record set (XML, JSON, or Struct) into a component.

  • trigger preSerialize

    System trigger that processes an occurrence before it is saved from a component into a disconnected record set (XML, JSON, or Struct).
